content.content: pflegen (weak, third-person singular present pflegt, past tense pflegte, past participle gepflegt, auxiliary haben)
From Middle High German phlëgen, from Old High German plëgan, from Proto-West Germanic *plehan.

content: providing care or service for someone/something
to nurse; to care for someone in poor health
to take care of, to tend to, to maintain
Er setzte sich faul hin und
to improve or care for something in an intellectual sense
to cultivate; to foster; to nurture; to maintain
to perform habitually; to be accustomed (to); to be in the habit (of)

content.content: Fuchs m (strong, genitive Fuchses, plural Füchse, diminutive Füchslein n or Füchschen n, feminine Füchsin)

From Middle High German vuhs, from Old High German fuhs, from Proto-West Germanic *fuhs, from Proto-Germanic *fuhsaz, from Proto-Indo-European *púḱsos (“the tailed one”), from *puḱ- (“tail”). Cognate with English fox, Sanskrit पुच्छ (púccha).

content: , du hast die Gans gestohlen. Gib sie wieder her!
, you've stolen the goose. Give it back here!
a clever or cunning person, sly fox
Unser Paul ist ja ein kleiner
the ace of diamonds in Doppelkopf, where it earns a side of players an extra point if they win it from the other side
Ich hatte nur vier Trümpfe und darunter beide
I had only four trumps and among them were both
a form of sunscald on hops

content.content: Herz n (weak, genitive Herzens or (very rare) Herzes, plural Herzen, diminutive Herzchen n or Herzlein n or ((also) Ruhrpöttisch) Herzken n)

From Middle High German hërze, from Old High German hërza, from Proto-West Germanic *hertā, from Proto-Germanic *hertô (“heart”), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱḗr (“heart”).
Cognate with Dutch hart, English heart, Danish hjerte, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌹𐍂𐍄𐍉 (hairtō).
Fahrer m (strong, genitive Fahrers, plural Fahrer, feminine Fahrerin)

From Middle High German von(e) (“from”), from Old High German fon(a), alongside rarer northern fan(a). It is uncertain whether the High German o-vocalism continues an original Proto-Germanic *funē, comparable with Sanskrit पुनः- (punaḥ-), or whether it is an atonic alteration of Proto-West Germanic *fanā. In the latter case, possibly from Proto-Germanic *afa + *ana (equivalent to German ab + an). Cognate with Old Dutch fan (modern van), Old Saxon fan(a) (Low German van), Old Frisian fon, fan, all “from”.

content: of, belonging to (often replacing genitive; see usage note below)
dem Nomine Substantivo, oder dem Hauptworte.
the substantive noun, or the [alternative term]. (headline)

content.content: 1796, Abraham Sahlstedt, Schwedische Grammatik nach dem Sprachgebrauch unserer Zeiten, Lübeck & Leipzig, page 259

content: welchem Geld soll ich als Arbeitsloser in Urlaub fahren?

content.content: From Middle High German base, from Old High German basa, from Proto-West Germanic *baswā, from Proto-Germanic *baswǭ (“father's sister; paternal aunt”). Compare Saterland Frisian Bääsje (“grandmother”), Dutch baas (“master; boss”). More at boss.
19th c., backformation from Basen, plural of Basis, from Latin basis, from Ancient Greek βάσις (básis).

content: base (compound that will neutralize an acid)
Schlecht gezahlt, bin ich doch freigebig und hilfsbereit
Badly paid, I am still generous and helpful
vis-à-vis, in dealings with, in dealing with, towards
